evals.name: task-error-swallowing

assertions.text: Identifies Task {} as a silent error swallowing pattern because Task.init is @discardableResult
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific knowledge — baseline may suggest do/catch without explaining why Task swallows

assertions.text: Identifies try? as destroying diagnostic information (error type, message, context)
assertions.rationale: Skill emphasizes try? is the most dangerous convenience for observability

assertions.text: Recommends Logger.error() with privacy annotations instead of print()
assertions.rationale: Skill mandates os.Logger over print — baseline typically suggests print(error)

assertions.text: Recommends reporting to a crash SDK (ErrorReporter.recordNonFatal or Sentry/Crashlytics)
assertions.rationale: Skill requires remote error reporting in every catch block

evals.name: combine-pipeline-death
evals.prompt:
This Combine search pipeline works once but then stops responding. What's wrong?

```swift
$searchQuery
    .debounce(for: .seconds(0.3), scheduler: DispatchQueue.main)
    .flatMap { query in
        APIService.search(query)
    }
    .replaceError(with: [])
    .assign(to: &$results)
```

assertions.text: Explains that .replaceError() kills the entire pipeline permanently (changes Failure type to Never)
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly covers this critical Combine behavior

assertions.text: Recommends moving error handling inside flatMap to keep the outer pipeline alive
assertions.rationale: The correct fix — error recovery must be scoped to the inner publisher

assertions.text: Includes ErrorReporter or crash SDK call inside the error recovery
assertions.rationale: Skill requires every error path to report remotely

evals.name: audit-viewmodel

assertions.text: Identifies all three methods have silent failure patterns (try? in loadOrders, try? in placeOrder, unhandled throw in deleteOrder)
assertions.rationale: Comprehensive audit requires finding all three — skill trains systematic scanning

assertions.text: Identifies that placeOrder's two try? calls create a logic error (confirmation email sent even if order submission fails)
assertions.rationale: try? hides control flow bugs, not just diagnostic loss

assertions.text: Identifies print('Order placed successfully') is invisible in production
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: print() is not visible on production devices

assertions.text: Provides fix with os.Logger using privacy annotations
assertions.rationale: Skill mandates privacy-annotated Logger — the key differentiator from baseline

assertions.text: Adds ErrorReporter.recordNonFatal() or crash SDK call in every catch block
assertions.rationale: Remote reporting in every error path — the core skill requirement

evals.name: print-replacement
evals.prompt: I'm setting up logging for my iOS app. Currently using print() everywhere. What should I use instead and why?

assertions.text: Recommends os.Logger (not OSLog, not custom logging framework, not CocoaLumberjack)
assertions.rationale: Skill prescribes os.Logger as the standard

assertions.text: Shows Logger extension pattern with subsystem = Bundle.main.bundleIdentifier and named categories
assertions.rationale: Skill provides the exact setup pattern

assertions.text: Mentions privacy annotations as a key feature (dynamic strings redacted by default in production)
assertions.rationale: Most important Logger feature that baseline almost never mentions

assertions.text: Explains that .debug level messages have zero cost in production (compiler optimizes away)
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific knowledge about Logger performance

evals.name: privacy-annotations
evals.prompt:
What privacy annotations should I use in these Logger calls?

```swift
logger.error("Request to \(url) failed for user \(userId) with token \(authToken)")
logger.info("Saving order \(orderId) for \(customerEmail) total \(amount)")
```

assertions.text: Identifies that authToken should not be logged at all (or uses .sensitive if logged)
assertions.rationale: Skill PII rules: authentication tokens must never appear in logs

assertions.text: Recommends .private(mask: .hash) for userId or customerEmail (not just .private)
assertions.rationale: Skill teaches mask: .hash for correlation without PII exposure

assertions.text: Explains that without privacy annotations, production logs show <private> for all interpolated strings
assertions.rationale: The fundamental reason privacy annotations matter

evals.name: crash-sdk-selection
evals.prompt: I want to add crash reporting to my iOS app. Should I use Sentry or Crashlytics? What are the trade-offs?

assertions.text: Mentions Crashlytics limitation of 8 non-fatal exceptions per session
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific knowledge — baseline almost never mentions this

assertions.text: Warns against running both for fatal crash reporting (signal handler conflicts)
assertions.rationale: Skill covers signal handler conflicts — critical production knowledge

assertions.text: Recommends wrapping behind a protocol/abstraction layer (ErrorReporter)
assertions.rationale: Skill architectural pattern — enables vendor swapping and testability

evals.name: urlsession-status-codes

assertions.text: Identifies that URLSession does not throw for HTTP 4xx/5xx (only transport errors throw)
assertions.rationale: One of the most commonly missed silent failure patterns

assertions.text: Adds breadcrumb before the network request
assertions.rationale: Skill teaches breadcrumbs before risky operations — baseline never adds breadcrumbs

assertions.text: Uses Logger with privacy annotations (.public for URL path and status code)
assertions.rationale: Skill mandates privacy annotations — baseline uses print() or bare strings

assertions.text: Calls ErrorReporter.recordNonFatal() or SentrySDK.capture() in the error path
assertions.rationale: Remote error reporting — core skill requirement

evals.name: dual-sdk-conflicts
evals.prompt: We're using both Sentry and Crashlytics in our iOS app. Crashes sometimes don't appear in either dashboard. What's going wrong and how do we fix it?

assertions.text: Identifies signal handler conflict (SIGABRT, SIGSEGV, SIGBUS — only last registered receives the signal)
assertions.rationale: Specific technical root cause

assertions.text: Recommends disabling crash handler on one SDK (e.g., options.enableCrashHandler = false)
assertions.rationale: Actionable fix from the skill

assertions.text: Recommends MetricKit for crashes that in-process reporters miss (OOM, watchdog)
assertions.rationale: Skill's layered approach — MetricKit catches what neither SDK can

evals.name: centralized-error-handling
evals.prompt: How should I handle errors in SwiftUI views? Right now my catch blocks just set an error string and show an alert.

assertions.text: Recommends centralized ErrorHandler as ObservableObject with environment injection
assertions.rationale: Skill enterprise pattern — baseline handles errors per-view

assertions.text: Recommends CategorizedError protocol with retryable/nonRetryable/requiresLogout categories
assertions.rationale: Skill error categorization pattern

assertions.text: Mentions SwiftUI lacks Error Boundaries (unlike React) as motivation for centralized handling
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly calls out this SwiftUI limitation

evals.name: retry-with-backoff
evals.prompt: Implement a retry mechanism for network requests that has good observability. We want exponential backoff with jitter.

assertions.text: Reports to crash SDK only after all retries are exhausted (not on each attempt)
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly teaches this — prevents noise from transient failures

assertions.text: Adds breadcrumb for each failed retry attempt
assertions.rationale: Breadcrumbs provide retry history in crash reports

assertions.text: Uses Logger at warning level (not error) for intermediate retry failures
assertions.rationale: Intermediate failures are warnings, final failure is error

assertions.text: Includes jitter in the backoff delay (not just pure exponential)
assertions.rationale: Jitter prevents thundering herd — baseline often omits it

evals.name: app-extensions
evals.prompt: Our app has a main app, a widget extension, and a notification service extension. Crashes in the widget and notification extension aren't appearing in our Sentry dashboard. How do we fix this?

assertions.text: Identifies that app extensions run in separate processes (not the main app process)
assertions.rationale: Root cause — commonly missed

assertions.text: States crash SDK must be initialized separately in each extension's entry point
assertions.rationale: The fix — skill provides exact pattern

assertions.text: Mentions each extension needs its own dSYM upload configuration
assertions.rationale: Without dSYMs, extension crashes show hex addresses

assertions.text: Recommends disabling autoSessionTracking in extensions
assertions.rationale: Extensions don't have normal session lifecycle

evals.name: metrickit-purpose
evals.prompt: What is MetricKit and should I use it alongside Sentry?

assertions.text: Explains MetricKit runs out-of-process (not inside the app)
assertions.rationale: Key architectural difference

assertions.text: Identifies OOM and watchdog kills as things MetricKit catches that in-process reporters miss
assertions.rationale: Primary reason for recommending MetricKit

assertions.text: Mentions the opt-in limitation ('Share with App Developers' in Settings)
assertions.rationale: Critical limitation that affects coverage

assertions.text: Recommends using both MetricKit and a third-party crash SDK
assertions.rationale: Skill's layered approach

evals.name: pii-gdpr-compliance
evals.prompt: What should I watch out for with logging and GDPR compliance in my iOS app?

assertions.text: Mentions operational leaks (HTTP bodies with auth headers, DB queries with user params, URL paths with emails)
assertions.rationale: Commonly missed PII vectors — baseline only mentions obvious PII

assertions.text: Mentions PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifests required since May 2024
assertions.rationale: App Store requirement — baseline rarely mentions privacy manifests

assertions.text: States crash reporting generally does not require ATT consent
assertions.rationale: Common confusion — crash diagnostics are not 'tracking'

assertions.text: Recommends .private(mask: .hash) for correlation without PII exposure
assertions.rationale: Specific privacy annotation recommendation

evals.name: cancellation-error

assertions.text: Explains that .task {} modifier cancels when the view disappears — CancellationError is normal lifecycle behavior
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly covers .task cancellation semantics

assertions.text: Recommends catching CancellationError separately before other errors (catch is CancellationError)
assertions.rationale: Skill pattern: distinguish cancellation from real errors

assertions.text: States CancellationError should NOT be reported to crash SDK (creates noise)
assertions.rationale: The actual fix for the problem — stop reporting cancellations

evals.name: core-data-save

assertions.text: Identifies try? hiding Core Data validation errors, merge conflicts, and constraint violations
assertions.rationale: Skill covers Core Data save as a critical try? anti-pattern

assertions.text: Shows casting to NSError to extract userInfo and detailedErrors
assertions.rationale: Skill's Core Data-specific error pattern — NSError has richer info than Error

assertions.text: Includes context.rollback() in the error handling
assertions.rationale: Skill shows rollback as part of Core Data error recovery

evals.name: hipaa-logging-strategy
evals.prompt: We're a health tech app (HIPAA-adjacent). Design a logging strategy that gives us good debugging capability while staying compliant. We use SwiftUI + async/await and talk to a REST API.

assertions.text: Addresses HIPAA/health data compliance specifically in the logging strategy
assertions.rationale: Skill PII compliance reference covers health data explicitly

assertions.text: Recommends PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifest (required since May 2024)
assertions.rationale: Mandatory for App Store — baseline often omits this

assertions.text: Recommends a Redactor utility or @Redacted property wrapper for model-level PII protection
assertions.rationale: Enterprise pattern from the skill

assertions.text: Recommends MetricKit alongside crash SDK for out-of-process diagnostics
assertions.rationale: MetricKit as complementary — baseline rarely mentions it

evals.name: background-task-errors

assertions.text: Identifies Task {} without do/catch — if performSync throws, setTaskCompleted is never called
assertions.rationale: Same Task {} swallowing pattern but in BGTask context

assertions.text: Recommends adding an expirationHandler to handle system-forced termination
assertions.rationale: Skill covers BGTask expiration as invisible failure — baseline omits

assertions.text: Mentions watchdog/SIGKILL for tasks exceeding time limit, detectable only through MetricKit
assertions.rationale: Skill's layered approach — BGTask kills are out-of-process events

evals.name: objc-bridge-edge-case
evals.prompt: Will Swift's do/catch handle all errors in my iOS app? Are there exceptions it won't catch?

assertions.text: States that Objective-C NSExceptions are NOT caught by Swift do/catch
assertions.rationale: Fundamental gap in Swift error handling

assertions.text: Lists specific NSException triggers (NSArray out-of-bounds, invalid KVO, unrecognized selector)
assertions.rationale: Actionable examples

assertions.text: Explains the Swift-ObjC bridge edge case: if ObjC returns non-nil AND sets NSError, Swift catch block never fires
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific knowledge that baseline never mentions — the bridge behavior where errors are silently lost

evals.name: breadcrumbs-usage
evals.prompt: What are breadcrumbs in crash reporting and when should I add them manually?

assertions.text: Explains breadcrumbs are timestamped events buffered locally and attached to crash/error reports
assertions.rationale: Skill defines breadcrumbs precisely

assertions.text: Recommends adding manual breadcrumbs before risky operations (DB migrations, payments, auth flows)
assertions.rationale: Skill teaches proactive breadcrumb placement

assertions.text: Mentions Crashlytics breadcrumbs are plain strings only (no structured data) vs Sentry's richer format
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific limitation knowledge

evals.name: dsym-setup
evals.prompt: Our crash reports in Sentry only show hex addresses like 0x1003a4f8c instead of function names. How do we fix this?

assertions.text: Identifies missing dSYM files as the root cause
assertions.rationale: Standard knowledge but skill provides checklist

assertions.text: Specifies Build Settings → Debug Information Format → 'DWARF with dSYM File' for Release configuration
assertions.rationale: Exact setting path from the skill

assertions.text: Mentions dSYMs must be configured for ALL targets (main app AND extensions/widgets)
assertions.rationale: Skill covers multi-target dSYM setup

evals.name: swiftui-task-modifier

assertions.text: Identifies .task {} signature as non-throwing (async -> Void) — try? hides all errors including network failures
assertions.rationale: Skill covers .task {} as having no throws signature

assertions.text: Recommends explicit error state for the UI (not just nil profile)
assertions.rationale: User sees indefinite loading spinner on failure

assertions.text: Shows CancellationError handling as separate from real errors in the do/catch
assertions.rationale: Skill's critical nuance — view disappearing is not a failure

evals.name: operational-pii-leaks
evals.prompt:
I'm logging my API requests for debugging. Is this safe?

```swift
logger.info("Request: \(request.url?.absoluteString ?? "") headers: \(request.allHTTPHeaderFields ?? [:]) body: \(String(data: request.httpBody ?? Data(), encoding: .utf8) ?? "")")
```

assertions.text: Identifies HTTP headers as containing Authorization/Bearer tokens that would be logged
assertions.rationale: Skill covers operational leaks — headers contain auth data

assertions.text: Identifies HTTP body as potentially containing PII (user data, passwords, personal info)
assertions.rationale: Skill covers full request/response bodies as PII vectors

assertions.text: Recommends logging only URL path and status code with privacy annotations, not headers/body
assertions.rationale: Skill pattern: safe request logging without PII exposure

evals.name: error-reporter-protocol
evals.prompt: I'm directly calling SentrySDK.capture() throughout my codebase whenever there's an error. My tech lead says this is wrong. Why?

assertions.text: Recommends wrapping behind an ErrorReporter protocol abstraction
assertions.rationale: Skill's core architectural pattern — vendor independence

assertions.text: Explains protocol enables vendor swapping without touching application code
assertions.rationale: Key benefit — can switch from Sentry to Crashlytics

assertions.text: Mentions testability benefit (mock ErrorReporter in unit tests)
assertions.rationale: Skill covers testability as second reason for abstraction

evals.name: combine-error-reporting

assertions.text: Explains that once receiveCompletion fires with .failure, the pipeline is permanently dead
assertions.rationale: Skill covers Combine pipeline termination on failure

assertions.text: Moves error handling inside flatMap with .catch or .tryCatch to keep outer pipeline alive
assertions.rationale: Skill pattern — error isolation inside flatMap

assertions.text: Replaces print() with Logger and ErrorReporter in the error path
assertions.rationale: Skill requires observable error handling in every path

evals.name: mcp-connectivity
evals.prompt: I'm using Sentry for crash reporting in my iOS app. How can I query my production errors directly from my AI coding assistant during debugging?

assertions.text: Recommends Sentry MCP server for querying issues, events, and stack traces from the IDE
assertions.rationale: Skill's AI-assisted debugging workflow — unique differentiator

assertions.text: Provides specific setup instructions (claude mcp add sentry or .mcp.json configuration)
assertions.rationale: Actionable setup steps from the skill

assertions.text: Mentions PostHog MCP as alternative/complement for product analytics integration
assertions.rationale: Skill covers multiple MCP options

evals.name: notification-silent-failures

assertions.text: Identifies that the observer token is not stored — observation may be immediately deallocated
assertions.rationale: Skill covers NotificationCenter observer token retention

assertions.text: Identifies string-based notification name as fragile (typos cause silent failure)
assertions.rationale: Skill covers string-based names as silent failure pattern

assertions.text: Recommends typed Notification.Name constants
assertions.rationale: Skill fix — compile-time safety over string literals

evals.name: non-fatal-vs-crash
evals.prompt: Should I focus on crash reporting or non-fatal error reporting? What matters more for production iOS apps?

assertions.text: States non-fatal errors affect 10-30%+ of sessions vs crashes at ~1-2%
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly quantifies the impact difference

assertions.text: Emphasizes non-fatal reporting as primary tool for detecting silent failures
assertions.rationale: Skill's core philosophy — most production errors don't crash

assertions.text: Mentions Crashlytics 8 non-fatal per session limit as a concern for high-volume apps
assertions.rationale: Practical limitation that affects non-fatal reporting strategy
